title: Pele and the Sacred Anger of Volcanoes
dek: *The Hawaiian fire goddess Pele offers a radical reframe for anyone taught that anger is ugly or dangerous. Her volcanic nature shows us that suppressed heat doesn't disappear but accumulates until it fractures the surface, while acknowledged rage becomes the force that creates islands, clears dead wood, and makes room for what wants to be born in you.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Pele, the Hawaiian volcano goddess, embodies anger as a sacred creative force rather than a destructive emotion to suppress. Her mythology teaches that rage held underground builds pressure until it erupts, while honored anger transforms landscapes and births new ground. Learning to work with volcanic anger means channeling its heat into boundaries, art, and necessary change before the pressure becomes unbearable.

She stood at the kitchen counter chopping vegetables for dinner, nodding along as her sister described yet another favor she needed, another weekend borrowed, another boundary gently crossed. The knife moved in steady rhythm. Her face remained pleasant. She heard herself say "of course, no problem" in a voice that sounded like hers but came from somewhere automatic, somewhere that had learned long ago to keep the peace. Only her jaw knew the truth. It had been clenched for twenty minutes, a small island of tension in an otherwise compliant body. She didn't register it until her sister left and she found herself standing alone in the kitchen, vegetables forgotten, aware of a headache blooming at her temples and a heat in her chest that had nowhere to go. This is the moment when Pele might have something to teach us. ## The Goddess Who Refused Containment In Hawaiian tradition, Pele is not a metaphor. She is the living presence in every volcanic eruption, every lava flow that reshapes the Big Island's coastline, every new acre of black rock cooling into future soil. Her stories are not gentle. She is jealous, passionate, destructive, fiercely protective of what is hers. She transforms entire landscapes overnight. She creates and destroys with the same molten hand. Western psychology has spent decades trying to help us manage anger, contain it, express it appropriately, channel it constructively. These are useful skills. But Pele's mythology offers something deeper: the recognition that some forces are not meant to be managed but honored. That the heat you feel is not a problem to solve but a power source you've been taught to fear. In my own practice, I've watched people describe their anger as if it were a foreign substance, something that invaded them, something shameful that proves they are not evolved or spiritual or kind. They apologize for feeling it before they've even named what provoked it. This is what happens in a culture that has demonized one of our most essential survival emotions, that has confused rage with violence, heat with harm. Pele does not apologize. When her sister Namakaokaha'i floods her volcanic fires, Pele erupts. When she is betrayed, she burns. When she loves, she pours herself out in rivers of molten stone that birth new earth. Her anger is inseparable from her creative power. ## What Happens Underground Beverly Engel's work on anger and boundaries illuminates what many of us experience but cannot name: that chronic niceness is often a trauma response, a learned behavior from environments where expressing anger was dangerous. She notes that people who suppress anger regularly often develop what she calls "anger debt," an accumulated backlog of unexpressed boundary violations that eventually demands payment. The volcano metaphor is precise here. Magma doesn't disappear when it's held underground. It accumulates. Pressure builds. The earth itself swells and cracks. Small earthquakes ripple outward. Animals sense the coming eruption and leave the area. The body knows before the mind does. You might notice this as chronic tension in your shoulders, a tightness in your throat that never fully releases, digestive issues that doctors can't explain, or a kind of background irritability that colors everything. The NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system documents how suppressed anger keeps the body in a state of low-grade activation, cortisol elevated, muscles braced for action that never comes. In our experience working with these patterns, the moment someone begins to name their anger is often when they first realize how much energy they've been spending holding it down. It's exhausting to be your own dam. Pele's mythology suggests a different approach: that the heat wants to move, and your job is not to contain it but to create channels where it can flow without destroying everything in its path. ## The Creative Force of Volcanic Heat Thomas Moore writes about anger as soul-making, a force that defines the borders of self and other, that says "this far and no further." Without anger, he suggests, we become diffuse, permeable, unable to maintain the boundaries that make intimacy possible. We become all things to all people and nothing much to ourselves. Pele creates islands. This is not incidental to her mythology but central. Her eruptions are not only destructive but generative. Where lava flows, new land appears. The Hawaiian islands themselves are Pele's children, born from the heat of her body meeting the resistance of the sea. The violence of the meeting creates something that did not exist before. Your anger, when you let yourself feel it fully, does similar work. It shows you where your boundaries have been crossed, what you value enough to defend, what matters so much that its violation creates heat in your body. This is information you cannot get any other way. Niceness obscures these borders. Rage illuminates them in fire. Over the years I've seen people describe the moment they first allowed themselves to feel their anger without immediately apologizing for it or trying to make it smaller. They use words like "alive," "clear," "powerful," "finally real." One woman said it felt like waking up after years of sleepwalking. Another said it was like finding a part of herself she'd been told didn't exist. The practice here is not to perform anger or act it out on others. Pele's eruptions are not personal attacks. They are the earth doing what the earth does when pressure builds beyond what the structure can hold. Your work is to notice the pressure before the eruption, to create outlets that honor the heat without requiring destruction. ## Lava Flows and Boundaries When lava meets the ocean, the meeting is violent. Steam explodes upward. The water boils. But the lava cools into new rock, extending the shoreline, creating tide pools where new life will establish itself. The boundary between fire and water becomes the birthplace of land. Your boundaries work the same way. They are not walls but living edges where you meet the world, where your heat meets other people's needs and desires, where negotiation happens. Without anger, you have no way to know when these edges are being violated. You become the ocean that simply absorbs whatever flows into it, heating up incrementally, never quite registering that you are being asked to hold too much. Soraya Chemaly's research on women and anger documents how socialization teaches many of us to mistake boundary violations for requests we should accommodate. We learn to read our own anger as evidence that we are being difficult, demanding, not nice enough. We learn to prioritize other people's comfort over our own integrity. This is not kindness. This is self-abandonment. Pele offers a different model. She does not ask permission to erupt. She does not check whether her lava flows are convenient for the villages below. She is a force of nature doing what her nature requires. This sounds dangerous until you realize that trying to suppress a volcano is far more dangerous than allowing it to flow through established channels. The practical work here, which you can explore more deeply through [the assessments hub](/assessments), involves learning to notice the early warning signs. The tightness in your chest. The forced smile. The moment you hear yourself saying yes when something in you is screaming no. These are the tremors before the eruption. This is your body trying to tell you that pressure is building. ## A Five-Minute Practice: Feeling the Heat You can do this right now, wherever you are reading this. Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Close your eyes if that feels safe, or soften your gaze toward the floor. Take three slow breaths, just noticing what you notice. Now bring to mind a recent moment when you felt even a flicker of irritation, frustration, or anger. Don't choose your biggest rage. Start small. Someone cutting you off in traffic. A comment that landed wrong. A request that felt like too much. Notice where you feel it in your body. Does your jaw tighten? Does your chest contract? Do your hands want to form fists? Does heat rise in your face? Stay with the sensation for thirty seconds without trying to change it or understand it. Just feel the heat. Now imagine that heat as lava, as molten rock that wants to move. Not at anyone. Not destructively. Just as a substance that has its own nature and wants to flow. Where does it want to go? Down into the earth? Out through your hands? Up and out through the crown of your head? Let yourself imagine the flow without censoring it. Take three more breaths. Open your eyes. Notice what you notice. This practice, which draws on somatic approaches detailed across the articles we've published on this site, is not about acting on anger but about developing a felt relationship with it. About learning its texture and temperature in your body so you can recognize it before it accumulates into something that demands a more dramatic release. ## The Offerings We Bring to Fire In Hawaiian tradition, people bring offerings to Pele. Gin, because she loves it. Ti leaves. Stones wrapped in cloth. These offerings are not bribes but acknowledgments. They say: we see you, we know you are here, we recognize your power. What would it mean to bring offerings to your own anger? To acknowledge it not as a problem but as a presence, a force that lives in you and deserves recognition? For some people this looks like creating a physical outlet. The [Plant Therapy Organic Lavender (30 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00LABX3DY?tag=spankyspinola-20) can create a ritual space where you allow yourself to feel what you feel without immediately needing to process or explain it. For others it means movement: dancing, running, shaking, letting the body discharge the energy before it calcifies into chronic tension. For others still it means art, writing, any form that lets the heat move through you into something external. The point is not catharsis but channel. Not explosion but flow. Pele's lava follows the path of least resistance. It moves downhill. It finds the cracks and openings. Your anger works the same way when you stop trying to dam it up. It will find an outlet. The question is whether you create that outlet consciously or whether it creates itself through your body, your relationships, your health. Some people find that working with warming botanicals helps create a felt sense of safely contained fire in the body. The [Solgar 5-HTP 100 mg (90 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00IAJJQHQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support the neurotransmitter systems that help regulate emotional intensity, though this is always a conversation to have with someone who knows your full picture. The point is not to numb the heat but to build enough internal capacity that you can feel it without it overwhelming your system. ## When the Old Ground Must Burn Pele's fires clear the land. They burn away the old growth, the dead wood, the structures that have outlived their purpose. This is terrifying if you are attached to what is burning. This is liberation if you understand that nothing new can grow in ground that is already full. Your anger often arrives at thresholds, at moments when something must change but you have been trying to maintain the old structure through sheer will. The relationship that stopped feeding you years ago. The job that drains you. The pattern of saying yes when you mean no. The identity you built that no longer fits who you are becoming. The heat you feel is not trying to destroy your life. It is trying to clear ground for what wants to come next. But you have to let the fire do its work. You have to stop trying to save what is already dead. This is where many people panic. They feel the heat rising and immediately try to suppress it, afraid of what might happen if they let themselves feel the full force of their rage. They are right to be afraid. Something will end. The old ground will burn. But Pele's mythology is clear on this point: the land that emerges after the fire is richer, more alive, capable of supporting growth that the old exhausted soil could not sustain. In my own practice, the people who make the most significant shifts are often the ones who finally let themselves get angry about things they have been tolerating for years. Not performing anger. Not using it as a weapon. But finally, finally admitting that something is not okay, has never been okay, and they are done pretending otherwise. This might mean having conversations you have been avoiding. It might mean leaving situations you have been trying to fix. It might mean disappointing people who are used to you being accommodating. The [Banyan Botanicals Vata Massage Oil (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0D9PD5J9L?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help ground the nervous system through these transitions, offering physical reassurance while your emotional world reorganizes itself. ## The New Islands Forming Here is what Pele's mythology promises: that the destruction is not the end of the story. That new land is forming even as the old burns. That the violence of transformation contains the seeds of what comes next. When you finally let yourself feel your anger, when you stop apologizing for it and start listening to what it is trying to tell you, something shifts. Not overnight. Not without grief for what must be released. But gradually you will notice that you have more energy. That your body feels more like yours. That you can sense your boundaries clearly and defend them without guilt. That you know what you want and what you will not tolerate. This is the new ground. This is the island forming. It is raw at first, black rock cooling, not yet covered in green. But it is yours. It is solid. It is land that did not exist before you allowed the heat to do its work. The journey through anger to clarity is one we explore more fully at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus is on reclaiming the full range of your emotional and spiritual power. But the principle is simple: that you cannot bypass the heat. You cannot skip to the peaceful island without first allowing the volcanic eruption that creates it. ## Tending Your Own Volcano So what does this look like in practice? How do you honor Pele without burning down your life? First, you learn to recognize the early signs. The jaw tension. The forced cheerfulness. The yes that feels like swallowing glass. These are not character flaws. They are your body trying to tell you that something is wrong, that a boundary has been crossed, that pressure is building. Second, you create outlets before the pressure demands them. This is where tools like [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) become practical. You need ways to feel and move the heat that don't require dramatic confrontation or relationship rupture. You need practices that let you discharge the energy before it accumulates into something that overwhelms your capacity to contain it skillfully. Third, you stop treating your anger as the problem. The problem is not that you feel rage. The problem is what provoked it. The violation, the disrespect, the repeated boundary crossing, the situation that is slowly killing your spirit. Your anger is the messenger trying to tell you this. Stop shooting the messenger. Fourth, you practice discernment. Not all anger requires external action. Sometimes the work is internal, the recognition that you have been expecting someone to be different than they are, that you have been trying to control what is not yours to control. But sometimes the anger is pointing to something that genuinely needs to change, and the work is finding the courage to change it. This is not a linear process. You will suppress when you mean to express. You will explode when you meant to channel. You will apologize for anger that deserved no apology. This is part of learning to work with a force you have been taught to fear. Be patient with yourself. Pele did not learn to create islands overnight. ## Three Questions People Ask About Sacred Anger ### Is it spiritual to feel this angry? Yes. Absolutely yes. The question itself reveals how deeply we have internalized the idea that spirituality means transcending difficult emotions rather than integrating them. Pele is a goddess. Her anger is sacred. Your anger, when it arises in defense of your integrity, your boundaries, your right to exist fully, is equally sacred. Spiritual bypassing, the attempt to skip over anger into premature forgiveness and peace, is not enlightenment. It is self-abandonment dressed up in spiritual language. The path through anger is more honest than the path around it. ### What if my anger hurts people I love? The distinction here is crucial. Feeling anger does not hurt anyone. Anger is an internal experience, a physiological response to perceived threat or violation. What can hurt people is how you express it: through verbal abuse, physical violence, or emotional manipulation. But these are not anger. These are unskillful attempts to discharge anger without taking responsibility for it. The work is to feel the heat fully in your own body and then choose consciously how and whether to communicate about what provoked it. You can be angry and kind. You can set fierce boundaries and remain compassionate. These are not opposites. ### How do I know if I'm suppressing anger or if I'm genuinely not angry? Your body knows. If you never feel angry but frequently experience jaw tension, headaches, digestive issues, chronic fatigue, or a sense of numbness or disconnection, your body is likely holding anger that your conscious mind has learned to suppress. If you find yourself suddenly erupting over small things after weeks of being fine, that is accumulated pressure finding an outlet. Genuine non-anger feels spacious, relaxed, easeful in the body. Suppressed anger feels like holding your breath, like bracing against something, like a tightness you have learned not to notice. The assessments available through [the about page](/about) can help you begin to map your relationship with anger more clearly. ## The Ground Still Cooling The Hawaiian islands are still forming. Kilauea has been erupting almost continuously for decades. New rock cools into new land. Ferns push through cracks in stone that was molten yesterday. This is not a metaphor for something else. This is the actual process by which earth creates itself, by which what is underground becomes landscape, becomes the ground you can stand on. Your anger is doing the same work. Not metaphorically. Actually. It is showing you where the old ground is exhausted, where new structure needs to form, where you have been standing on terrain that can no longer support your weight. The heat is not the problem. The heat is the force that will create the new ground, if you let it. This requires courage. It requires the willingness to let some things burn. It requires trusting that you will not be consumed by the fire that moves through you, that you are larger than any single emotion, that the heat is in service of something your conscious mind cannot yet see. Pele stands in the crater of Kilauea, hair streaming, eyes bright with flame. She is not sorry. She is not trying to be smaller or quieter or more convenient. She is doing what her nature requires, creating and destroying in the same breath, making room for what wants to be born. You carry that same fire. The question is whether you will spend your life trying to contain it or whether you will learn to let it flow, to create the channels where it can move without destroying what matters, to trust that the heat you feel is not evidence of your failure but proof of your aliveness, your refusal to disappear, your sacred and volcanic right to take up space in the world. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on pele and the sacred anger of volcanoes, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
